video_script.title: The Fire That Never Reaches Denver Is Already in Your Lungs
video_script.hook: Two new studies reveal how wildfire smoke travels atmospheric highways thousands of miles from ignition points, carrying particulates that bypass…
body_slides: Sealed windows. Duct-taped fans. August indoors.
Morning AQI checks. Like weather. But different.
The app shows numbers. From fires burning elsewhere.
video_script.voiceover_text: September 5th, Denver had America's worst air quality. No fires burned within hundreds of miles of the city. Sealed windows. Duct-taped fans. August indoors. Morning AQI checks. Like weather. But different. The app shows numbers. From fires burning elsewhere. The fire that never reaches you is already inside. That's The Docket. Follow for more stories like this.
video_script.cta:
Follow @docketclimate for more
Climate news · Adaptation · Speculation
Link in bio
video_script.voiceover_cta: That's The Docket. Follow for more stories like this.
image_prompts: A child's hands pressing duct tape around a box fan filter against a sealed window, afternoon light filtering through hazy glass, portrait orientation
A smartphone screen showing an AQI app with a red hazardous reading, held against the backdrop of a clear Denver skyline with mountains barely visible through haze
Empty playground equipment in a Denver neighborhood park, swings motionless in thick smoky air, natural lighting casting muted shadows

video_script.title: The Woman Who Sells Phoenix Back Its Own Desert
video_script.hook: Desiree Quintero operates in a Phoenix where residential water allocation follows tier restrictions that residents monitor like commodity prices.
body_slides: Desiree Quintero's business card reads 'Native Plant Specialist.'
Her storage yard: acres of palo verde, ocotillo, barrel cactus.
Plants she harvests from construction sites. Before bulldozers arrive.
video_script.voiceover_text: Phoenix residents track water tiers like gas prices. Tier 3 restrictions make traditional lawns functionally illegal. Desiree Quintero's business card reads 'Native Plant Specialist.'. Her storage yard: acres of palo verde, ocotillo, barrel cactus. Plants she harvests from construction sites. Before bulldozers arrive. Phoenix pays premium prices for what it paved over. More at The Docket. Follow to keep seeing these.
video_script.cta:
Follow @docketclimate for more

Aerial view of organized rows of native cacti and desert plants in a commercial nursery yard, mountains visible in hazy distance
Construction bulldozer positioned at edge of untouched Sonoran desert, saguaro cacti standing in foreground like sentinels
